technologies.name: Writing
technologies.positive: The substrate of schooling itself: literacy restructures cognition, enabling analysis, abstraction and cumulative critique (Ong, 1982).
technologies.negative: Offloads memory to external storage; people retain where to find information rather than the information itself (Sparrow et al., 2011).
technologies.notes: Memory atrophy and hollow understanding; the fear is now fully inverted, since we panic when students cannot write.

technologies.name: Printing press
technologies.positive: Cheap identical texts made the textbook, the graded classroom, the standardized curriculum and mass literacy schooling possible (Eisenstein, 1979).
technologies.negative: Entrenched textbook orthodoxy and the recitation culture that dominated classrooms for a century (Cuban, 1993).
technologies.notes: Order fear realized (Reformation, religious wars); enabled the textbook, the graded classroom and mass literacy schooling.

technologies.name: Novels
technologies.positive: Fiction became the core of literacy education; free voluntary reading is among the best-supported literacy practices (Krashen, 2004).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; the feared idleness and displacement of serious reading were never validated (Vogrinčič, 2008).
technologies.notes: Health framing was medical: nervous overexcitement and reading mania. The feared object became the English curriculum.

technologies.name: Railways
technologies.positive: Indirect: enabled school consolidation and excursions, and fed the standardized time discipline behind the bell schedule (Tyack, 1974).
technologies.negative: None substantive for learning; the feared mental harms were largely press amplification (Milne-Smith, 2016).
technologies.notes: Railway spine and railway madness; education effects indirect only (consolidation, excursions, bell-schedule time discipline).

technologies.name: Telegraph
technologies.positive: Marginal: vocational telegraphy training and current-events pedagogy; mainly precursor infrastructure (Standage, 1998).
technologies.negative: Genealogical rather than direct: originated context-free news, information detached from the capacity to act on it (Postman, 1985).
technologies.notes: Nervous strain of accelerated life (neurasthenia) and wire fraud; education role was precursor infrastructure only.

technologies.name: Telephone
technologies.positive: Administrative infrastructure only: attendance, parent contact, later homework hotlines (Fischer, 1992).
technologies.negative: None; the feared social harms were empirically refuted, and no learning claim existed (Fischer, 1992).
technologies.notes: Improper intimacy and eroded visiting; minor germ and shock worries. Became school admin infrastructure, not a learning tool.

technologies.name: Movies
technologies.positive: Launched instructional film, the visual instruction movement, and educational technology as a professional field (Saettler, 1990).
technologies.negative: Modest measured harms: disturbed children's sleep and shifted attitudes (Payne Fund studies; Charters, 1933).
technologies.notes: Payne Fund measured attitudes and sleep loss; crime imitation central. Founded instructional film and media literacy.

technologies.name: Radio
technologies.positive: Established broadcast distance education; Schools of the Air made schooling possible for remote children (Cuban, 1986).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; child radio-addiction claims were never validated (Orben, 2020).
technologies.notes: Child addiction claims were medically framed; the propaganda fear was realized in adults. Schools of the Air faded; distance education endured.

technologies.name: Comic books
technologies.positive: Rehabilitated as literacy scaffolds: graphic novels support reluctant, struggling and EAL readers (Krashen, 2004).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; the literacy-harm case rested on fabricated clinical evidence (Tilley, 2012).
technologies.notes: Literacy panic plus Wertham's psychiatric claims, later shown fabricated. Graphic novels are now classroom texts.

technologies.name: Television
technologies.positive: Designed educational TV produced large early-learning gains, greatest for disadvantaged children (Bogatz & Ball, 1971; Kearney & Levine, 2019).
technologies.negative: Heavy leisure viewing associated with lower achievement beyond a modest threshold, partly via homework displacement (Williams et al., 1982).
technologies.notes: The analog-era peak on every axis; the biggest measured education finding was positive (Sesame Street). Design, not the screen, drives learning.

technologies.name: Rock and roll
technologies.positive: Marginal: popular music eventually entered music curricula through informal-learning pedagogy (Green, 2008).
technologies.negative: None; the panic was moral and behavioural, with no learning claim (Cohen, 1972).
technologies.notes: Morals, manners and concert disorder, never marks; later absorbed into music curricula and taught as a moral-panic case study.

technologies.name: Pocket calculator
technologies.positive: Freed mathematics teaching for concepts and problem solving; improved attitudes without harming overall skills when instruction adapted (Hembree & Dessart, 1986).
technologies.negative: Real skill shift: sustained early-grade calculator use hindered basic computation development (Hembree & Dessart, 1986).
technologies.notes: Marked * on this timeline. The purest learning fear here: arithmetic collapse. Settled by assessment redesign (calculator and non-calculator papers).

technologies.name: Home video (VHS)
technologies.positive: Model classroom adoption: cheap, reliable and teacher-controlled; ancestor of on-demand instructional video (Cuban, 1986).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; the content panic rested on weak and partly manufactured evidence (Barker, 1984).
technologies.notes: Video nasties content panic with psychological-harm and copycat claims; the same device became a model classroom technology.

technologies.name: Personal computer
technologies.positive: Word processing measurably improved student writing; computing became a school subject (Bangert-Drowns, 1993).
technologies.negative: Oversold and underused: heavy investment produced limited classroom change and little measured learning gain (Cuban, 2001).
technologies.notes: Marked * on this timeline. Dominant fear inverted: exclusion, not exposure. Hacker panic on the order side; oversold and underused for learning.

technologies.name: Dungeons and Dragons
technologies.positive: Absorbed co-curricularly for narrative literacy, probability, collaboration and social-skill development (Bowman, 2010).
technologies.negative: None; the suicide and dissociation claims collapsed under scrutiny (Laycock, 2015).
technologies.notes: Occult and suicide claims collapsed under scrutiny; now used co-curricularly for literacy, probability and social skills.

technologies.name: Video games
technologies.positive: Well-designed digital games outperform non-game instruction on average; design features moderate everything (Clark et al., 2016).
technologies.negative: Time displacement is real: heavier play associated with less homework and reading among adolescents (Cummings & Vandewater, 2007).
technologies.notes: Homework displacement and addiction real for heavy users; the violence linkage was central but weakly supported. Well-designed games outperform non-game instruction.

technologies.name: Internet
technologies.positive: Open resources and online/blended learning that modestly outperforms face-to-face alone; transformed access to information (Means et al., 2010).
technologies.negative: Access alone produced no learning gains, and intensive classroom use predicted worse performance (Cristia et al., 2017; OECD, 2015).
technologies.notes: Fears on every axis, several realized; contract cheating industrialized. Transformation huge, measured learning gains poor.

technologies.name: Social media
technologies.positive: Teacher professional learning networks and student voice; digital citizenship entered curricula (Carpenter & Krutka, 2014).
technologies.negative: Quasi-experimental evidence of harm to student mental health, degrading readiness to learn (Braghieri et al., 2022).
technologies.notes: Teen mental health is the core claim, with causal magnitude contested; schools became the front line regardless of where the science lands.

technologies.name: Smartphones
technologies.positive: Teacher-controlled uses: physics sensors and data loggers, instant translation for EAL students (Vogt & Kuhn, 2012).
technologies.negative: Mere presence taxes cognitive capacity; restrictions raised exam results, most for low achievers (Ward et al., 2017; Beland & Murphy, 2016).
technologies.notes: Best-evidenced learning fear on this timeline (in-class distraction); drove the global ban wave, the sharpest policy reversal in modern education.

technologies.name: Generative AI
technologies.positive: Guardrailed, teacher-mediated tutoring produced large gains in randomized trials (De Simone et al., 2025; Kestin et al., 2025).
technologies.negative: Unguarded access reduced skill acquisition: practice gains but worse unassisted exam performance (Bastani et al., 2025).
technologies.notes: Cheating overstated, offloading unresolved, deepfake abuse realized; assessment redesign already underway.

technologies.name: Paper
technologies.positive: Cheap writing surfaces made copybooks, written exercises and paper examinations economically possible: the material precondition of mass written schooling (Kurlansky, 2016).
technologies.negative: None for learning; the durability fear concerned legal records, and archives adapted (Kurlansky, 2016).
technologies.notes: Frederick II's decrees (1221, 1231) barred paper for official documents as impermanent; the fear was record integrity, not learning.

technologies.name: Hindu–Arabic numerals
technologies.positive: Positional notation made written arithmetic teachable at scale; Italian abbaco schools built the first mass commercial-mathematics curriculum (Van Egmond, 1980).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; the forgery risk in ledgers was procedural and receded with standard bookkeeping (Menninger, 1969).
technologies.notes: Florence barred Arabic numerals in bankers' records (1299) on forgery grounds; the zero-as-satanic story is a modern myth.

technologies.name: Handwriting technologies (slates to ballpoints; typewriter)
technologies.positive: Slates, cheap steel pens and copybooks made universal writing practice affordable; typewriting became vocational and later core curriculum (Thornton, 1996).
technologies.negative: Real skill shift: each tool eroded the previous script culture, though core literacy was unaffected (Thornton, 1996).
technologies.notes: Caution: the circulating list of dated 'teacher complaints' about slates, ink and ballpoints is apocryphal; the documented worry lives in penmanship journals and 1940s–50s ballpoint resistance.

technologies.name: Chess
technologies.positive: Absorbed as a co-curricular thinking game; meta-analysis finds only modest, largely near-transfer academic benefits (Sala & Gobet, 2016).
technologies.negative: None; the 1859 harm claims were never substantiated, and the later 'chess makes you smarter' saviour claim is equally overstated (Sala & Gobet, 2016).
technologies.notes: Scientific American (1859) condemned the chess craze as wasting young minds during the Morphy boom; devil and saviour claims both failed, the whole chronology in miniature.

technologies.name: Penny dreadfuls and dime novels
technologies.positive: Mass cheap fiction recruited working-class boys into regular reading, building the juvenile readership later literacy work stood on (Springhall, 1998).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; magistrates and coroners blamed dreadfuls for juvenile crime without evidence (Springhall, 1998).
technologies.notes: The direct ancestor of the comic-book panic; the education response was curated 'wholesome reading' lists and youth library collections.

technologies.name: Phonograph and player piano
technologies.positive: Records created the music-appreciation curriculum and early recorded language instruction (Katz, 2004).
technologies.negative: Real shift: domestic amateur music-making declined as playback replaced performance, though causality is entangled with broader leisure change (Katz, 2004).
technologies.notes: Sousa's 'The Menace of Mechanical Music' (1906) is the first clean offloading-of-a-practised-skill panic, ancestor of the calculator and AI worries.

technologies.name: Sunday comic strips
technologies.positive: Built mass child reading habits and the visual-verbal literacy that graphic-novel pedagogy later drew on (Gordon, 1998).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; the taste-corruption case never produced learning evidence (Gordon, 1998).
technologies.notes: 'A Crime Against American Children' (Ladies' Home Journal, 1909); the first comics panic, three decades before Wertham and the comic-book entry.

technologies.name: Crossword puzzles
technologies.positive: Word puzzles settled into vocabulary and spelling practice across subjects and languages (Danesi, 2002).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; the craze faded into a durable literate pastime (Danesi, 2002).
technologies.notes: New York Times editorials (1924) called the puzzle 'a primitive sort of mental exercise'; the Times adopted it in 1942, completing the inversion.

technologies.name: Automation ('cybernation')
technologies.positive: The scare financed vocational education and retraining (MDTA 1962; Vocational Education Act 1963) and reopened the purpose-of-schooling debate (Grubb & Lazerson, 2004).
technologies.negative: None direct; its durable legacy is anchoring education policy to labour-market panic cycles (Grubb & Lazerson, 2004).
technologies.notes: The Triple Revolution memorandum (1964); the direct ancestor of the AI-and-jobs framing now shaping education policy.

technologies.name: Photocopier
technologies.positive: The duplicated worksheet and course pack made teacher-made materials universal, loosening the textbook's monopoly on classroom content (Cuban, 1993).
technologies.negative: Real but bounded: worksheet pedagogy entrenched low-level seatwork, and easy copying fed the academic-integrity lineage (Cuban, 1993; Eaton, 2021).
technologies.notes: Williams & Wilkins v. United States (1973) settled the copyright fear; Eaton's integrity-panic lineage runs print, photocopier, word processor, AI.

technologies.name: Walkman
technologies.positive: Personal audio normalized portable language tapes and audiobooks, an early personalized-learning channel (du Gay et al., 1997).
technologies.negative: None substantiated for learning; the isolation critique was cultural, and hearing risk was the durable health finding (du Gay et al., 1997).
technologies.notes: Bloom (1987) made it a symbol of sealed-off youth; the direct ancestor of earbuds-in-class and 'alone together' smartphone fears.

technologies.name: Pagers
technologies.positive: Nothing substantive; the parent emergency-contact argument later central to phone debates was first rehearsed here (period press, 1988–1993).
technologies.negative: None substantiated; bans policed drug-culture symbolism as much as classroom disruption (period press and state statutes, 1988–1993).
technologies.notes: The first pocket communication device banned from schools; the policy template for the smartphone-ban wave three decades later.

technologies.name: Mobile-phone, Wi-Fi and 5G radiation
technologies.positive: Wireless networking underpinned connected classrooms and 1:1 device programs, whatever their measured returns (OECD, 2015).
technologies.negative: None established: reviews find no demonstrated cognitive or health harm at exposure limits; IARC's 2B 'possible' label reflects limited evidence (IARC, 2013; WHO, 2014).
technologies.notes: Ignited by the Reynard lawsuit on Larry King Live (1993); school Wi-Fi removal campaigns and 5G arson followed. Distinct from the smartphone attention row in the source matrix.

technologies.name: Virtual reality
technologies.positive: Immersive simulation shows modest average learning gains, strongly moderated by instructional design (Merchant et al., 2014).
technologies.negative: Little substantiated; headset age minimums are precautionary, and developmental evidence is thin either way (Bailey & Bailenson, 2017).
technologies.notes: A panic that keeps arriving before the technology does: 1990s dissociation fears, then metaverse child-safety alarm (2021–22).

technologies.name: Texting and instant messaging
technologies.positive: Corrective studies found textism use correlates positively with phonological awareness and literacy (Plester et al., 2009; Crystal, 2008).
technologies.negative: None for literacy; the sexting harms were real but belong to safety, not learning (Crystal, 2008).
technologies.notes: The 2003 'exam essay written in text-speak' story that anchored the panic traces to a single unverified case; treat it as folklore.

technologies.name: Wikipedia
technologies.positive: Expert review found accuracy comparable to Britannica; it became the default background-reading layer and a core information-literacy teaching object (Giles, 2005).
technologies.negative: Real but bounded: sourcing worries were legitimate, and open text eased copy-paste plagiarism (Head & Eisenberg, 2010).
technologies.notes: Middlebury's citation ban (2007); settled into 'start there, don't cite it', a plausible template for AI-use norms.

technologies.name: GPS navigation
technologies.positive: GPS and GIS entered geography and science fieldwork, enabling data-rich local studies (Kerski, 2003).
technologies.negative: Habitual GPS use is associated with poorer spatial memory; causal direction unresolved (Dahmani & Bohbot, 2020).
technologies.notes: The cleanest single-skill offloading case after the calculator; a natural citation for the age-factor module.

technologies.name: Search engines
technologies.positive: Instant retrieval transformed homework and inquiry; search strategy became an explicitly taught skill (Head & Eisenberg, 2010).
technologies.negative: Transactive-memory shift: people remember where to find information rather than the information itself (Sparrow et al., 2011).
technologies.notes: Carr's 'Is Google Making Us Stupid?' (2008) is the bridge from older media fears to the AI offloading debate.

technologies.name: 3D printing
technologies.positive: Anchored the school makerspace movement, with engagement and design-thinking gains that are strongly implementation-dependent (Blikstein, 2013).
technologies.negative: Real but manageable: desktop printers emit ultrafine particles, a ventilation issue in classrooms (Stephens et al., 2013).
technologies.notes: The gun panic never touched classrooms; the education story is makerspaces, and the health note is practical rather than moral.

technologies.name: Smart speakers and voice assistants
technologies.positive: Voice assistants opened hands-free question-asking and accessibility affordances for young and print-impaired learners (Lovato et al., 2019).
technologies.negative: Little substantiated; the command-style-talk and outsourced-answers worries remain unmeasured (Lovato et al., 2019).
technologies.notes: The 'Alexa generation' manners alarm produced Amazon's Magic Word feature (2018); the Furby restrictions (1999) are the surveillance-fear precursor.

technologies.name: Fidget spinners
technologies.positive: A regulated place persists for simpler sensory tools in special-education practice (Stalvey & Brasell, 2006).
technologies.negative: The marketed attention benefit reversed under test: spinners impaired attention in children with ADHD (Graziano et al., 2020).
technologies.notes: The purest classroom micro-panic: marketed as an attention aid, banned as an attention destroyer, full cycle inside a year.

technologies.name: Spectacles
technologies.positive: Extended reading lives by decades and widened who could study and teach; presbyopia stopped ending scholarly careers (Ilardi, 2007).
technologies.negative: None; among the least contested technologies ever adopted (Ilardi, 2007).
technologies.notes: Invented c. 1285 in northern Italy; the printing boom made reading glasses a mass market. A reminder that access technologies can transform learning without a single recorded fear.

technologies.name: Blackboard and chalk
technologies.positive: Enabled whole-class simultaneous instruction and cheap shared display; hailed in 1841 as ranking its inventor 'among the greatest benefactors of mankind' (Bumstead, 1841; Krause, 2000).
technologies.negative: None; later critics complain only that pedagogy froze around it (Krause, 2000).
technologies.notes: Coulson's quip that the last dramatic instructional innovation 'occurred while Thomas Jefferson was president' names the chalkboard, c. 1801. The inverse of a panic: instant, celebrated, universal adoption.

technologies.name: Braille
technologies.positive: Gave blind students direct literacy rather than mediated listening, transforming education for the blind worldwide (Mellor, 2006).
technologies.negative: None; the 'War of the Dots' was a professional dispute between rival tactile codes, not a fear of the technology (Mellor, 2006).
technologies.notes: Invented by a 15-year-old student (1824); officially adopted in France in 1854. Assistive technologies as a class attract essentially no panic.

technologies.name: Penny post and correspondence education
technologies.positive: Cheap uniform postage made education-at-a-distance a mass product, from Pitman's shorthand lessons (1840s) to International Correspondence Schools' millions of enrolments (Moore & Kearsley, 2011).
technologies.negative: Little for learning; fringe diploma-mill fraud shadowed the sector without discrediting it (Moore & Kearsley, 2011).
technologies.notes: The origin of distance education; every later remote-learning technology (radio, TV, internet, videoconferencing) inherits its model.

technologies.name: Pencil with attached eraser
technologies.positive: Cheap, erasable, sharpenable writing put low-stakes practice and revision in every hand; the attached eraser normalized visible error correction (Petroski, 1990).
technologies.negative: Trace of a worry: some nineteenth-century teachers argued erasers would encourage carelessness, a grumble that never scaled (Petroski, 1990).
technologies.notes: Later the required instrument of machine-scored testing (the No. 2 pencil), tying the humblest technology on this sheet to the most consequential one.

technologies.name: The school science laboratory
technologies.positive: The Bunsen burner (1855), cheap glassware and student benches made experiment a school activity rather than a demonstration, institutionalized by the heuristic movement (DeBoer, 1991).
technologies.negative: None at introduction; the durable critique is cost and cookbook practicals, a design problem rather than a fear (DeBoer, 1991).
technologies.notes: A transformation panic never touched: hands-on science entered schooling with saviour rhetoric only.

technologies.name: Mimeograph and spirit duplicators
technologies.positive: Stencil and spirit duplicators gave teachers cheap in-house publishing decades before xerography: the worksheet, the class newsletter, the exam paper (Proudfoot, 1972).
technologies.negative: None recorded; its legacy critique is the same seatwork worry later attached to the photocopier (Proudfoot, 1972; Cuban, 1993).
technologies.notes: The purple ditto page is the ancestor of the photocopied worksheet economy; adoption was silent and total.

technologies.name: Electric school clocks and bells
technologies.positive: Master-clock and bell systems synchronized mass schooling, making the period, the timetable and the bell schedule possible (Tyack, 1974).
technologies.negative: None contemporaneously; the deep critique of factory time-discipline in schooling came generations later (Tyack, 1974).
technologies.notes: The railway's time discipline entering the building; arguably the most structural technology on this sheet, adopted without comment.

technologies.name: Slide rule (in schooling)
technologies.positive: The standard computational instrument of STEM schooling for most of a century; slide-rule fluency structured physics and engineering curricula (Stoll, 2006).
technologies.negative: None; it died in about five years after 1972 without ever having been feared (Stoll, 2006).
technologies.notes: The inverse case study: no panic at arrival, and the panic at its departure attached to its replacement, the calculator.

technologies.name: School building services (lighting, heating, cooling)
technologies.positive: Lighting, heating, ventilation and cooling measurably condition learning: hot school years reduce learning, and air conditioning offsets most of the loss (Park et al., 2020).
technologies.negative: None; the finding runs the other way, that missing infrastructure quietly harms achievement (Park et al., 2020).
technologies.notes: The least glamorous row and among the best evidenced: infrastructure beats gadgets, and nobody has ever panicked about a boiler.

technologies.name: Filmstrip and slide projectors
technologies.positive: Cheap, teacher-paced visual media that actually fit classroom routines, unlike the film reels they simplified (Saettler, 1990).
technologies.negative: None; the format quietly delivered what instructional film had promised (Saettler, 1990; Cuban, 1986).
technologies.notes: The beep-and-advance filmstrip is Cuban's fit principle embodied: teacher control, low cost, zero panic.

technologies.name: Machine test scoring (IBM 805 to Scantron)
technologies.positive: The IBM 805 (1937) and Scantron (1972) made mass objective testing economical, enabling standardized assessment systems at national scale (Lemann, 1999).
technologies.negative: Real but professional: machine-scorable formats tilted assessment toward selected response, a pedagogical critique that never became a public fear (Lemann, 1999).
technologies.notes: Among the most consequential education technologies of the century and among the least feared; today's anxiety belongs to testing policy, not the machine.

technologies.name: The yellow school bus
technologies.positive: Standardized in 1939 (the national minimum-standards conference that fixed the yellow), busing made school consolidation and rural secondary schooling possible (Tyack, 1974).
technologies.negative: None for learning; long ride times in consolidated districts are the quiet cost (Tyack, 1974).
technologies.notes: A transformation of access on the railway's scale with none of the railway's dread.

technologies.name: Overhead projector
technologies.positive: The exemplar of classroom-fit technology: face-the-class projection, teacher-made transparencies, no darkened room; near-universal adoption by the 1980s (Cuban, 1986).
technologies.negative: None; its failure mode was transparency-driven lecturing, a pedagogy critique (Cuban, 1986).
technologies.notes: Cuban's contrast case for film, radio and TV: the technology teachers chose, rather than the ones chosen for them.

technologies.name: Language laboratory
technologies.positive: NDEA funding (1958) built audio labs nationwide, institutionalizing listening and drill in language teaching (Salaberry, 2001).
technologies.negative: Its own disappointment: audiolingual drill underdelivered and labs decayed, the hype half of Cuban's cycle without the panic half (Salaberry, 2001).
technologies.notes: A saviour claim that failed quietly; useful as the control case where neither devil nor miracle materialized.

technologies.name: Educational programming environments (Logo to Scratch)
technologies.positive: Constructionist environments from Logo (1967) to Scratch (2007) made programming a school literacy and seeded today's computing curricula (Papert, 1980).
technologies.negative: None feared; measured transfer from programming to general problem-solving proved weaker than the rhetoric (Pea & Kurland, 1984).
technologies.notes: Pure saviour framing for four decades; the panic about code arrived only when AI began writing it.

technologies.name: Assistive and accessibility technologies
technologies.positive: From the Kurzweil reading machine (1976) to screen readers, captioning and text-to-speech, assistive technology moved disabled students toward inclusion, generalized as universal design for learning (Rose & Meyer, 2002).
technologies.negative: None; the category is effectively panic-free (Rose & Meyer, 2002).
technologies.notes: The strongest counter-pattern on the sheet: technologies framed as access are welcomed; technologies framed as youth leisure are feared.

technologies.name: Probeware and microcomputer-based labs
technologies.positive: Real-time sensors and microcomputer-based labs measurably improved conceptual learning in physics, among the best-evidenced classroom technologies (Thornton & Sokoloff, 1990).
technologies.negative: None; cost and calibration are the only complaints on record (Thornton & Sokoloff, 1990).
technologies.notes: A teacher-controlled, curriculum-embedded technology with strong evidence and zero fear: the anti-panic profile in pure form.

technologies.name: Classroom response systems (clickers to Kahoot)
technologies.positive: Response systems turned lectures interactive and normalized whole-class formative assessment, from Classtalk through peer instruction to Kahoot (Mazur, 1997).
technologies.negative: None; the gamified descendants draw only engagement-versus-substance quibbles (Mazur, 1997).
technologies.notes: Formative-assessment machinery adopted through teachers; the panic never came because students never controlled it.

technologies.name: Interactive whiteboards
technologies.positive: Interactive whiteboards reached near-saturation in UK classrooms within a decade, carrying digital display into ordinary teaching (Higgins et al., 2007).
technologies.negative: Measured learning gains were marginal relative to cost; reviews find the board changes presentation more than pedagogy (Higgins et al., 2007).
technologies.notes: A billion-pound quiet purchase: the clearest modern case of transformation-scale spending with neither panic nor much measured payoff.

technologies.name: Learning management systems
technologies.positive: Learning management systems became schooling's administrative nervous system: assignments, submission, feedback and records in one place, from Blackboard (1997) to Google Classroom (2014) (Watson & Watson, 2007).
technologies.negative: None publicly; the professional worries are workload, lock-in and the quiet datafication of students (Watson & Watson, 2007).
technologies.notes: Probably the most-used education technology on Earth, and almost never discussed outside the profession.

technologies.name: Plagiarism-detection services
technologies.positive: Similarity-checking industrialized integrity enforcement and, used formatively, teaches citation practice (Eaton, 2021).
technologies.negative: Professional critique rather than fear: false confidence in scores, student IP objections (A.V. v. iParadigms, 2009), and policing displacing integrity teaching (Eaton, 2021).
technologies.notes: The inversion that proves the pattern: a technology created in response to a panic generates none itself, because institutions rather than students wield it.

technologies.name: Interactive science simulations (PhET)
technologies.positive: Free research-based simulations became standard physics and chemistry teaching tools worldwide, with conceptual gains comparable or superior to equipment labs for many targets (Wieman et al., 2008).
technologies.negative: None; the live professional debate is when simulations should replace rather than precede hands-on work (Wieman et al., 2008).
technologies.notes: Founded by a Nobel physicist; hundreds of millions of runs a year and not one recorded fear.

technologies.name: Chromebooks and cloud classroom suites
technologies.positive: Cheap managed laptops plus cloud suites made 1:1 provision affordable and collaborative documents routine, taking a majority of US school device purchases within five years (Singer, 2017).
technologies.negative: Bounded: student-data privacy complaints and lock-in concerns, raised by advocates rather than the public (Krutka et al., 2021).
technologies.notes: The largest device transformation since the textbook, arriving to almost no public comment; the privacy critique stayed professional.

technologies.name: Machine translation in the language classroom
technologies.positive: Instant translation lowered barriers for EAL students and families, and pushed language teaching toward tasks translation cannot do (Ducar & Schocket, 2018).
technologies.negative: Professional worry, not panic: unedited machine translation as unauthorized aid in language classes, prefiguring the AI-writing debate (Ducar & Schocket, 2018).
technologies.notes: The quiet dress rehearsal for generative AI in one subject: the same integrity questions, none of the public alarm.

technologies.name: Videoconferencing and emergency remote teaching
technologies.positive: Made system-wide remote schooling possible at days' notice in 2020, and normalized hybrid parent conferences and remote guest expertise thereafter (Hodges et al., 2020).
technologies.negative: Emergency remote teaching underperformed classrooms, but the documented harm belongs to closure and circumstance rather than to the tool (Hodges et al., 2020).
technologies.notes: The Partial fears: 'Zoom fatigue' claims, Zoombombing incidents, and learning-loss alarm that targeted school closure, not videoconferencing itself.

Dates on the timeline.definition: Approximate year the technology came into being, in the form relevant to this timeline (first working or public version).
Approximate year it began having a big societal impact: mass adoption, or the visibility that triggered the panic.

Writing's impact date is anchored to the Greek literacy context of Plato's panic. The internet's origin counts ARPANET (1969), with the web (1991) noted. Novels are dated as the 18th-century mass-market form.

Impact summaries.definition: Brief evidence-based summary of how the technology improved or transformed education, with a short in-text citation. Full citations are on the References page.
Brief evidence-based summary of documented harm or hindrance to learning. 'None' entries mean no substantiated harm; the cited study supports that conclusion (e.g. a debunking or refutation).

Fear ratings (Yes / Partial / No).definition: Contemporaries' claims at the time — not whether the fear later proved justified.
Fear clearly articulated at meaningful scale during the panic period.
Fear present but marginal, contested, or narrowly framed (judgment calls; see Notes in each technology's detail view).
Not a feature of the panic.

Category definitions.definition: Claimed harm to memory, attention, reasoning, literacy, skill formation, academic integrity, or study time.
Corruption of character: sexual permissiveness, idleness, occult influence, collapse of manners, delinquent values.
Claimed physical or mental health harm, including medically framed addiction, sleep, eyesight, and psychiatric claims.
Crime, violence, riots, propaganda, fraud, radicalization, or political destabilization.

Transformed education.definition: Durable structural or pedagogical change to how schooling is organized, taught, or assessed.
Niche or marginal absorption into schooling.

Dial (0–5).definition: Education-related fear intensity at historical peak (intensity multiplied by reach), shown on each technology's fear dial. Measures the size of the fear, not its accuracy.

Each dial is a retrospective judgment call, not a computed statistic. The score synthesizes contemporaneous claims, their scale and persistence, and how directly they targeted learning or cognitive development — weighted by historical reach at the panic's peak. Related codings (fear type, engines, control) use the same judgment-based method.

What's included.definition: Eighteen technologies drawn from historical scholarship on technology panics and education.

Later additions — including the pocket calculator, personal computer, and technologies from the expanded matrix — broaden the timeline beyond the original panic set. Some dial scores for these are assigned retrospectively.

Timeline display.definition: Technologies with documented education-related public panics — the entries this project originally tracked.
Quiet transformers: technologies that measurably changed schooling without a meaningful associated panic.

fearColumns.desc: Claimed harm to memory, attention, reasoning, literacy, skill formation, academic integrity, or study time.
Corruption of character: sexual permissiveness, idleness, occult influence, collapse of manners, delinquent values.
Claimed physical or mental health harm, including medically framed addiction, sleep, eyesight, and psychiatric claims.
Crime, violence, riots, propaganda, fraud, radicalization, or political destabilization.

meta.title: Technology Panics & Education
meta.subtitle: How fears shaped — and transformed — learning across four millennia
schemaNotes: Personal computer has learning fear = yes but engines = [] because its learning fear was exclusion (fear of not having it), unique in the dataset.
Home video (VHS) is its own natural experiment: the same device was a moral panic at home and a model classroom technology at school.
